paper_title: Heavy Mineral and Carbonate Content Analysis in Upper Rhine Graben Sediments

methods.description: This method involves analyzing the heavy mineral composition of Pliocene and Pleistocene sediments to characterize fluvial fine sands from the Rhine system. The analysis provides information on the sediment source and weathering.

steps.description: 171 samples from the Viernheim research borehole were analyzed for their heavy mineral content.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze heavy mineral content of sediment samples

steps.description: Sodium polywolframate with a density of 2.85 g/cm³ was used as the heavy liquid to separate the heavy from the light fraction in a centrifuge.
steps.searchable_summary: separate heavy and light mineral fractions using sodium polywolframate
outputs: separated heavy and light mineral fractions

steps.description: Samples were boiled with concentrated hydrochloric acid to remove iron and manganese hydroxide crusts.
steps.searchable_summary: remove iron and manganese hydroxide crusts using hydrochloric acid

methods.description: This method involves determining the carbonate content in sand samples from the Viernheim research borehole to understand the calcareous nature of the sediments.

steps.description: The samples were initially ground up in a mortar before being ground down to ca. 100 μ in a ball mill.
steps.searchable_summary: grind samples to fine powder using mortar and ball mill

steps.description: The carbonate content was determined using the method described by Scheibler (DIN ISO 10693), which involved two to three measurements.
steps.searchable_summary: determine carbonate content using Scheibler method

steps.description: The results were used to derive an average number.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate average carbonate content from measurements
